title: Couscous with chicken and chickpeas
subtitle: Moroccan spiced chicken and chickpea stew served over steamed couscous.
excerpt: Couscous with chicken is the Friday dish of Moroccan home cooking: chicken thighs braised in a spiced tomato and chickpea stew, served over a mound of steamed couscous that has absorbed the sauce. Turmeric, cumin, and coriander provide the spice base; fresh coriander finishes the dish. An hour from start to table.
sourceNotes: Couscous with chicken is one of the defining dishes of Moroccan home cooking, traditionally eaten on Fridays after midday prayers. Documented across Moroccan culinary literature throughout the twentieth century. The chickpea variation is particularly associated with the cities of Fez and Casablanca. The couscous here is steamed by soaking in boiling water and finishing with butter, rather than the traditional multiple-steam method, which produces a comparable result for a home cook.

content.text: Couscous in Moroccan cooking is not a grain to toss cold from a bowl. It is steamed until light and separate, then served under the stew so it absorbs the sauce from below. The quick method (boiling water poured over the couscous, covered and left to steam) produces a result close enough to the traditional double-steamed version that most cooks outside Morocco use it. The key is the final step: tossing the couscous with a knob of butter and breaking up any clumps before the stew goes on top.

The stew is a one-pan braise. Chicken thighs are browned, then cooked with onion, garlic, a can of tomatoes, and chickpeas in a spice base of turmeric, cumin, coriander, and paprika. Everything simmers together for forty minutes while the sauce reduces around the chicken. The chickpeas absorb the sauce and become soft without becoming mushy.

Season the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs generously with salt. Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide, heavy pan over medium-high heat. Working in batches, place the chicken skin-side down and cook for six minutes until the skin is golden. Turn and cook for two minutes. Transfer to a plate.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} sliced onion to the pan and cook for five minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}} garlic, turmeric, cumin, coriander, and paprika, and stir for one minute. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, the drained {{chickpeas-tinned}} chickpeas, and {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock. Season with salt.

Return the chicken to the pan, skin-side up. Bring to a gentle simmer, cover with a lid slightly ajar, and cook for thirty minutes. Remove the lid for the final ten minutes to reduce the sauce. The chicken should be cooked through and the sauce thickened around the chickpeas.

Put {{couscous}} of couscous in a large bowl with a pinch of salt. Pour over 360 ml of boiling water, the ratio is 1:1.2 by volume. Stir once, cover tightly with a plate or cling film, and leave for five minutes. Uncover, add {{unsalted-butter}} of butter, and use a fork to fluff the couscous and break up any clumps. Taste and add salt if needed.
Mound the couscous onto a large platter or into bowls. Spoon the chicken and chickpea stew over the top, making sure to include plenty of the sauce. Scatter the coriander over and serve immediately.

items.fix: Fluff with a fork immediately after adding the butter, while the couscous is still warm. Break up clumps by rubbing between your fingers.
items.cause: Too much water used, or not fluffed while still hot.
items.symptom: The couscous is clumped or gluey

items.fix: Reduce the heat and simmer more gently. Tinned chickpeas need only warming through, thirty minutes of gentle cooking is the limit before they start to break down.
items.cause: Cooked for too long at too high a heat.

items.fix: Remove the chicken and reduce the sauce over high heat for five minutes before returning.

content.text: Friday couscous is the meal that most Moroccan families eat after midday prayers. The traditional version involves a slow-steamed couscous in a special pot called a couscoussier, with the stew below and the grain above taking in the steam. The simplified version (boiling water absorption) is what most Moroccan home cooks use in France and the UK where couscoussiers are less available. The flavour difference is small. The dish itself is the same one that Moroccan families have been making for centuries: grain and braise together in one bowl, the sauce absorbed as you eat.
